{"content":"1: # Envoy tests
2: 
3: Envoy uses the [Google Test framework](https://github.com/google/googletest) for
4: tests, including
5: [Google Mock](https://github.com/google/googletest/blob/master/googlemock/README.md) for
6: mocks and matchers. See the documentation on those pages for information on the
7: various classes, macros, and matchers that Envoy uses from those frameworks.
8: 
9: ## Integration tests
10: 
11: Envoy contains an integration testing framework, for testing
12: downstream-Envoy-upstream communication.
13: [See the framework's README for more information.](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/test/integration/README.md)
14: 
15: ## Custom matchers
16: 
17: Envoy includes some custom Google Mock matchers to make test expectation
18: statements simpler to write and easier to understand.
19: 
20: ### HeaderValueOf
21: 
22: Tests that a HeaderMap argument contains exactly one header with the given key,
23: whose value satisfies the given expectation. The expectation can be a matcher,
24: or a string that the value should equal.
25: 
26: Examples:
27: 
28: ```cpp
29: EXPECT_THAT(response->headers(), HeaderValueOf(Headers::get().Server, "envoy"));
30: ```
31: 
32: ```cpp
33: using testing::HasSubstr;
34: EXPECT_THAT(request->headers(),
35:             HeaderValueOf(Headers::get().AcceptEncoding, HasSubstr("gzip")));
36: ```
37: 
38: ### HttpStatusIs
39: 
40: Tests that a HeaderMap argument has the provided HTTP status code. The status
41: code can be passed in as a string or an integer.
42: 
43: Example:
44: 
45: ```cpp
46: EXPECT_THAT(response->headers(), HttpStatusIs("200"));
47: ```
48: 
49: ### HeaderMapEqual and HeaderMapEqualRef
50: 
51: `HeaderMapEqualRef` tests that two `HeaderMap` arguments are equal.
52: `HeaderMapEqual` is the same, but it compares two pointers to `HeaderMap`s, and
53: the matcher's argument must be a `HeaderMapPtr`.
54: 
55: Example:
56: 
57: ```cpp
58: EXPECT_THAT(response->headers(), HeaderMapEqualRef(expected_headers));
59: ```
60: 
61: ### ProtoEq, ProtoEqIgnoringField, RepeatedProtoEq
62: 
63: Tests equality of protobufs, with a variant that ignores the value (including
64: presence) of a single named field. Another variant can be used to compare two
65: instances of Protobuf::RepeatedPtrField element-by-element.
66: 
67: Example:
68: 
69: ```cpp
70: envoy::service::discovery::v3::DeltaDiscoveryRequest expected_request;
71: // (not shown: set some fields of expected_request...)
72: EXPECT_CALL(async_stream_, sendMessage(ProtoEqIgnoringField(expected_request, "response_nonce"), false));
73: 
74: response->mutable_resources()->Add();
75: response->mutable_resources()->Add();
76: response->mutable_resources()->Add();
77: // (not shown: do something to populate those empty added items...)
78: EXPECT_CALL(callbacks_, onConfigUpdate(RepeatedProtoEq(response->resources()), version));
79: ```
80: 
81: ### IsSubsetOfHeaders and IsSupersetOfHeaders
82: 
83: Tests that one `HeaderMap` argument contains every header in another
84: `HeaderMap`.
85: 
86: Examples:
87: 
88: ```cpp
89: EXPECT_THAT(response->headers(), IsSubsetOfHeaders(allowed_headers));
90: EXPECT_THAT(response->headers(), IsSupersetOfHeaders(required_headers));
91: ```
92: 
93: ## Controlling time in tests
94: 
95: In Envoy production code, time and timers are managed via
96: [`Event::TimeSystem`](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/include/envoy/event/timer.h),
97: which provides a mechanism for querying the time and setting up time-based
98: callbacks. Bypassing this abstraction in Envoy code is flagged as a format
99: violation in CI.
100: 
101: In tests we use a derivation
102: [`Event::TestTimeSystem`](test_common/test_time_system.h) which adds the ability
103: to sleep or do a blocking timed wait on a condition variable. There are two
104: implementations of the `Event::TestTimeSystem` interface:
105: `Event::TestRealTimeSystem`, and `Event::SimulatedTimeSystem`. The latter is
106: recommended for all new tests, as it helps avoid flaky tests on slow machines,
107: and makes tests run faster.
108: 
109: Typically we do not want to have both real-time and simulated-time in the same
110: test; that could lead to hard-to-reproduce results. Thus both implementations
111: have a mechanism to enforce that only one of them can be instantiated at once.
112: A runtime assertion occurs if an `Event::TestRealTimeSystem` and
113: `Event::SimulatedTimeSystem` are instantiated at the same time. Once the
114: time-system goes out of scope, usually at the end of a test method, the slate
115: is clean and a new test-method can use a different time system.
116: 
117: There is also `Event::GlobalTimeSystem`, which can be instantiated in shared
118: test infrastructure that wants to be agnostic to which `TimeSystem` is used in a
119: test. When no `TimeSystem` is instantiated in a test, the `Event::GlobalTimeSystem`
120: will lazy-initialize itself into a concrete `TimeSystem`. Currently this is
121: `TestRealTimeSystem` but will be changed in the future to `SimulatedTimeSystem`.
122: 
123: 
124: ## Benchmark tests
125: 
126: Envoy uses [Google Benchmark](https://github.com/google/benchmark/) for
127: microbenchmarks. There are custom bazel rules, `envoy_cc_benchmark_binary` and
128: `envoy_benchmark_test`, to execute them locally and in CI environments
129: respectively. `envoy_benchmark_test` rules call the benchmark binary from a
130: [script](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/bazel/test_for_benchmark_wrapper.sh)
131: which runs the benchmark with a minimal number of iterations and skipping
132: expensive benchmarks to quickly verify that the binary is able to run to
133: completion. In order to collect meaningful bechmarks, `bazel run -c opt` the
134: benchmark binary target on a quiescent machine.
135: 
136: If you would like to detect when your benchmark test is running under the
137: wrapper, call
138: [`Envoy::benchmark::skipExpensiveBechmarks()`](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/test/benchmark/main.h).
139: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/envoy--v1.32.1@b35dd17bea856e19fc4e65fb05e68170841d3726/-/blob/test/README.md"}

{"content":"1: # Developer use of CI Docker images
2: 
3: There are two available flavors of Envoy Docker images for Linux, based on Ubuntu and Alpine Linux
4: and an image based on Windows2019.
5: 
6: ## Ubuntu Envoy image
7: 
8: The Ubuntu based Envoy Docker image at [`envoyproxy/envoy-build-ubuntu:<hash>`](https://hub.docker.com/r/envoyproxy/envoy-build/) is used for CI checks,
9: where `<hash>` is specified in [`envoy_build_sha.sh`](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/ci/envoy_build_sha.sh). Developers
10: may work with the latest build image SHA in [envoy-build-tools](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy-build-tools/blob/main/toolchains/rbe_toolchains_config.bzl#L8)
11: repo to provide a self-contained environment for building Envoy binaries and running tests that reflects the latest built Ubuntu Envoy image.
12: Moreover, the Docker image at [`envoyproxy/envoy:dev-<hash>`](https://hub.docker.com/r/envoyproxy/envoy/tags?name=dev) is an image that has an Envoy binary at `/usr/local/bin/envoy`.
13: The `<hash>` corresponds to the main commit at which the binary was compiled. Lastly, `envoyproxy/envoy:dev` contains an Envoy
14: binary built from the latest tip of main that passed tests.
15: 
16: ## Distroless Envoy image
17: 
18: Minimal images based on a [distroless](https://github.com/GoogleContainerTools/distroless) allow for quicker deployment of Envoy.
19: 
20: The Distroless base image is only built with symbols stripped.
21: 
22: ## Debug Envoy image
23: 
24: To get the binary with symbols, use the corresponding Ubuntu based debug image. The image is pushed with two different tags:
25: `<hash>` and `latest`. Parallel to the Ubuntu images above, `<hash>` corresponds to the
26: main commit at which the binary was compiled, and `latest` corresponds to a binary built from the latest tip of main that passed tests.
27: 
28: ## Windows 2019 Envoy image
29: 
30: On August 31, 2023 the Envoy project ended official Windows support due to a lack of resources.
31: We will continue to accept patches related to the Windows build. Until further notice, Windows
32: builds are excluded from Envoy CI, as well as the Envoy release and security processes.
33: 
34: The Windows 2019 based Envoy Docker image at [`envoyproxy/envoy-build-windows2019:<hash>`](https://hub.docker.com/r/envoyproxy/envoy-build-windows2019/)
35: is used for CI checks, where `<hash>` is specified in [`envoy_build_sha.sh`](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/ci/envoy_build_sha.sh).
36: Developers may work with the most recent `envoyproxy/envoy-build-windows2019` image to provide a self-contained environment for building Envoy binaries and
37: running tests that reflects the latest built Windows 2019 Envoy image.
38: 
39: # Build image base and compiler versions
40: 
41: Currently there are three build images for Linux and one for Windows:
42: 
43: * `envoyproxy/envoy-build` — alias to `envoyproxy/envoy-build-ubuntu`.
44: * `envoyproxy/envoy-build-ubuntu` — based on Ubuntu 20.04 (Focal) with GCC 9 and Clang 14 compiler.
45: * `envoyproxy/envoy-build-centos` — based on CentOS 7 with GCC 9 and Clang 14 compiler, this image is experimental and not well tested.
46: * `envoyproxy/envoy-build-windows2019` — based on Windows ltsc2019 with VS 2019 Build Tools, as well as LLVM.
47: 
48: The source for these images is located in the [envoyproxy/envoy-build-tools](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy-build-tools)
49: repository.
50: 
51: We use the Clang compiler for all Linux CI runs with tests. We have an additional Linux CI run with GCC which builds binary only.
52: 
53: # C++ standard library
54: 
55: As of November 2019 after [#8859](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/pull/8859) the official released binary is
56: [linked against libc++ on Linux](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/bazel/README.md#linking-against-libc-on-linux).
57: To override the C++ standard library in your build, set environment variable `ENVOY_STDLIB` to `libstdc++` or `libc++` and
58: run `./ci/do_ci.sh` as described below.
59: 
60: # Building and running tests as a developer
61: 
62: The `./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh` script can be used to set up a Docker container on Linux and Windows
63: to build an Envoy static binary and run tests.
64: 
65: The build image defaults to `envoyproxy/envoy-build-ubuntu` on Linux and
66: `envoyproxy/envoy-build-windows2019` on Windows, but you can choose build image by setting
67: `IMAGE_NAME` in the environment.
68: 
69: In case your setup is behind a proxy, set `http_proxy` and `https_proxy` to the proxy servers before
70: invoking the build.
71: 
72: ```bash
73: IMAGE_NAME=envoyproxy/envoy-build-ubuntu http_proxy=http://proxy.foo.com:8080 https_proxy=http://proxy.bar.com:8080 ./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh <build_script_args>
74: ```
75: 
76: Besides `http_proxy` and `https_proxy`, maybe you need to set `go_proxy` to replace the default GOPROXY in China.
77: 
78: ```bash
79: IMAGE_NAME=envoyproxy/envoy-build-ubuntu go_proxy=https://goproxy.cn,direct http_proxy=http://proxy.foo.com:8080 https_proxy=http://proxy.bar.com:8080 ./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh <build_script_args>
80: ```
81: 
82: To force the Envoy build image to be refreshed by Docker you can set `ENVOY_DOCKER_PULL=true`.
83: 
84: ```bash
85: ENVOY_DOCKER_PULL=true ./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh <build_script_args>
86: ```
87: 
88: 
89: # Generating compile commands
90: 
91: The `./ci/do_ci.sh` script provides a CI target that generates compile commands for clangd or other tools.
92: 
93: ```bash
94: ENVOY_GEN_COMPDB_OPTIONS="--vscode --exclude_contrib" ./ci/do_ci.sh refresh_compdb
95: ```
96: 
97: ## On Linux
98: 
99: An example basic invocation to build a developer version of the Envoy static binary (using the Bazel `fastbuild` type) is:
100: 
101: ```bash
102: ./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh './ci/do_ci.sh dev'
103: ```
104: 
105: The Envoy binary can be found in `/tmp/envoy-docker-build/envoy/source/exe/envoy-fastbuild` on the Docker host. You
106: can control this by setting `ENVOY_DOCKER_BUILD_DIR` in the environment, e.g. to
107: generate the binary in `~/build/envoy/source/exe/envoy-fastbuild` you can run:
108: 
109: ```bash
110: ENVOY_DOCKER_BUILD_DIR=~/build ./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh './ci/do_ci.sh dev'
111: ```
112: 
113: For a release version of the Envoy binary you can run:
114: 
115: ```bash
116: ./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh './ci/do_ci.sh release.server_only'
117: ```
118: 
119: The build artifact can be found in `/tmp/envoy-docker-build/envoy/source/exe/envoy` (or wherever
120: `$ENVOY_DOCKER_BUILD_DIR` points).
121: 
122: To enable the previous behavior of the `release.server_only` target where the final binary was copied to a tar.gz file
123: (e.g. envoy-binary.tar.gz), you can run:
124: 
125:   ```bash
126:   ./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh './ci/do_ci.sh release.server_only.binary'
127:   ```
128: 
129: For a debug version of the Envoy binary you can run:
130: 
131: ```bash
132: ./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh './ci/do_ci.sh debug.server_only'
133: ```
134: 
135: The build artifact can be found in `/tmp/envoy-docker-build/envoy/source/exe/envoy-debug` (or wherever
136: `$ENVOY_DOCKER_BUILD_DIR` points).
137: 
138: To leverage a [bazel remote cache](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/tree/main/bazel#advanced-caching-setup):
139: 1. add bazel options like `--remote_cache` and/or `--remote_cache_header` to a `.bazelrc` file, such as `user.bazelrc`. For example
140: 
141: ```bash
142: build:my-remote-cache --remote_cache=grpcs://remotecache.googleapis.com
143: build:my-remote-cache --remote_cache_header=Authorization="Bearer <token>"
144: ```
145: 
146: 2. specify the config in the `BAZEL_BUILD_EXTRA_OPTIONS` environment variable. Run
147: 
148: ```bash
149: export BAZEL_BUILD_EXTRA_OPTIONS=--config=my-remote-cache
150: ```
151: 
152: The `./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh './ci/do_ci.sh <TARGET>'` targets are:
153: 
154: * `api` — build and run API tests under `-c fastbuild` with clang.
155: * `asan` — build and run tests under `-c dbg --config=clang-asan` with clang.
156: * `asan <test>` — build and run a specified test or test dir under `-c dbg --config=clang-asan` with clang.
157: * `debug` — build Envoy static binary and run tests under `-c dbg`.
158: * `debug <test>` — build Envoy static binary and run a specified test or test dir under `-c dbg`.
159: * `debug.server_only` — build Envoy static binary under `-c dbg`.
160: * `docker` — build Docker images, expects `release` or `release.server_only` to have been run first.
161: * `dev` — build Envoy static binary and run tests under `-c fastbuild` with clang.
162: * `dev <test>` — build Envoy static binary and run a specified test or test dir under `-c fastbuild` with clang.
163: * `dev.contrib` — build Envoy static binary with contrib and run tests under `-c fastbuild` with clang.
164: * `dev.contrib <test>` — build Envoy static binary with contrib and run a specified test or test dir under `-c fastbuild` with clang.
165: * `release` — build Envoy static binary and run tests under `-c opt` with clang.
166: * `release <test>` — build Envoy static binaries and run a specified test or test dir under `-c opt` with clang.
167: * `release.server_only` — build Envoy static binaries under `-c opt` with clang.
168: * `sizeopt` — build Envoy static binary and run tests under `-c opt --config=sizeopt` with clang.
169: * `sizeopt <test>` — build Envoy static binary and run a specified test or test dir under `-c opt --config=sizeopt` with clang.
170: * `sizeopt.server_only` — build Envoy static binary under `-c opt --config=sizeopt` with clang.
171: * `coverage` — build and run tests under `-c dbg` with gcc, generating coverage information in `$ENVOY_DOCKER_BUILD_DIR/envoy/generated/coverage/coverage.html`.
172: * `coverage <test>` — build and run a specified test or test dir under `-c dbg` with gcc, generating coverage information in `$ENVOY_DOCKER_BUILD_DIR/envoy/generated/coverage/coverage.html`. Specify `//contrib/...` to get contrib coverage.
173: * `msan` — build and run tests under `-c dbg --config=clang-msan` with clang.
174: * `msan <test>` — build and run a specified test or test dir under `-c dbg --config=clang-msan` with clang.
175: * `tsan` — build and run tests under `-c dbg --config=clang-tsan` with clang.
176: * `tsan <test>` — build and run a specified test or test dir under `-c dbg --config=clang-tsan` with clang.
177: * `fuzz` — build and run fuzz tests under `-c dbg --config=asan-fuzzer` with clang.
178: * `fuzz <test>` — build and run a specified fuzz test or test dir under `-c dbg --config=asan-fuzzer` with clang. If specifying a single fuzz test, must use the full target name with "_with_libfuzzer" for `<test>`.
179: * `compile_time_options` — build Envoy and run tests with various compile-time options toggled to their non-default state, to ensure they still build.
180: * `compile_time_options <test>` — build Envoy and run a specified test or test dir with various compile-time options toggled to their non-default state, to ensure they still build.
181: * `clang_tidy <files>` — build and run clang-tidy specified source files, if no files specified, runs against the diff with the last GitHub commit.
182: * `check_proto_format`— check configuration, formatting and build issues in API proto files.
183: * `fix_proto_format`— fix configuration, formatting and build issues in API proto files.
184: * `check_and_fix_proto_format` — check and fix configuration, fomatting and build issues in API proto files.
185: * `format`— run validation, linting and formatting tools.
186: * `docs`— build documentation tree in `generated/docs`.
187: 
188: ## On Windows
189: 
190: An example basic invocation to build the Envoy static binary and run tests is:
191: 
192: ```bash
193: ./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh './ci/windows_ci_steps.sh'
194: ```
195: 
196: You can pass additional command line arguments to `./ci/windows_ci_steps.sh` to list specific `bazel` arguments and build/test targets.
197: as set environment variables to adjust your container build environment as described above.
198: 
199: The Envoy binary can be found in `${TEMP}\envoy-docker-build\envoy\source\exe` on the Docker host. You
200: can control this by setting `ENVOY_DOCKER_BUILD_DIR` in the environment, e.g. to
201: generate the binary in `C:\Users\foo\build\envoy\source\exe` you can run:
202: 
203: ```bash
204: ENVOY_DOCKER_BUILD_DIR="C:\Users\foo\build" ./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh './ci/windows_ci_steps.sh'
205: ```
206: 
207: Note the quotations around the `ENVOY_DOCKER_BUILD_DIR` value to preserve the backslashes in the
208: path.
209: 
210: If you would like to run an interactive session to keep the build container running (to persist your local build environment), run:
211: 
212: ```bash
213: ./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh 'bash'
214: ```
215: 
216: From an interactive session, you can invoke `bazel` directly, or use the `./ci/windows_ci_steps.sh` script to build and run tests.
217: Bazel will look for .bazelrc in the `${HOME}` path, which is mapped to the persistent path `${TEMP}\envoy-docker-build\` on the
218: Docker host.
219: 
220: # Testing changes to the build image as a developer
221: 
222: The base build image used in the CI flows here lives in the [envoy-build-tools](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy-build-tools)
223: repository. If you need to make and/or test changes to the build image, instructions to do so can be found in
224: the [build_container](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy-build-tools/blob/main/build_container/README.md) folder.
225: See the Dockerfiles and build scripts there for building a new image.
226: 
227: # macOS Build Flow
228: 
229: The macOS CI build is part of the [Azure Pipelines](https://dev.azure.com/cncf/envoy/_build) workflow.
230: Dependencies are installed by the `ci/mac_ci_setup.sh` script, via [Homebrew](https://brew.sh),
231: which is pre-installed on the [Azure Pipelines macOS image](https://github.com/actions/virtual-environments/blob/main/images/macos/macos-10.15-Readme.md).
232: The dependencies are cached and re-installed on every build. The `ci/mac_ci_steps.sh` script executes the specific commands that
233: build and test Envoy. Note that the full version of Xcode (not just Command Line Tools) is required.

{"content":"1: # Support tools
2: 
3: A collection of CLI tools meant to support and automate various aspects of
4: developing Envoy, particularly those related to code review. For example,
5: automatic DCO signoff and pre-commit format checking.
6: 
7: ## Usage
8: 
9: To get started, you need only navigate to the Envoy project root and run:
10: 
11: ```bash
12: ./support/bootstrap
13: ```
14: 
15: This will set up the development support toolchain automatically. The toolchain
16: uses git hooks extensively, copying them from `support/hooks` to the `.git`
17: folder.
18: 
19: The commit hook checks can be skipped using the `--no-verify` flags, as
20: so:
21: 
22: ```bash
23: git commit --no-verify
24: ```
25: 
26: You can also do this by adding `NO_VERIFY` to `.env`, for example:
27: 
28: ```console
29: $ echo NO_VERIFY=1 >> .env
30: ```
31: 
32: Or settting it in your environment:
33: 
34: ```console
35: $ export NO_VERIFY=1
36: ```
37: 
38: ## Functionality
39: 
40: Currently the development support toolchain exposes two main pieces of
41: functionality:
42: 
43: * Automatically appending DCO signoff to the end of a commit message if it
44:   doesn't exist yet. Correctly covers edge cases like `commit --amend` and
45:   `rebase`.
46: * Automatically running DCO and format checks on all files in the diff, before
47:   push.
48: 
49: [filter]: https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy-filter-example
50: 
51: ## Fixing Format Problems
52: 
53: If the pre-push format checks detect any problems, you can either fix the
54: affected files manually or run the provided formatting script.
55: 
56: To run the format fix script directly:
57: 
58: ```console
59: bazel run //tools/code_format:check_format -- fix && bazel run //tools/code:check -- fix -s main -v warn
60: ```
61: 
62: To run the format fix script under Docker:
63: 
64: ```console
65: ./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh './ci/do_ci.sh format'
66: ```
67: 
68: To run clang-tidy under Docker, run the following (this creates a full
69: compilation db and takes a long time):
70: 
71: ```console
72: ./ci/run_envoy_docker.sh ci/do_ci.sh clang_tidy
73: ```

{"content":"1: # C++ coding style
2: 
3: * The Envoy source code is formatted using clang-format. Thus all white spaces, etc.
4:   issues are taken care of automatically. The Azure Pipelines will automatically check
5:   the code format and fail. There are make targets that can both check the format
6:   (check_format) as well as fix the code format for you (fix_format). Errors in
7:   .clang-tidy are enforced while other warnings are suggestions. Note that code and
8:   comment blocks designated `clang-format off` must be closed with `clang-format on`.
9:   To run these checks locally, see [Support Tools](support/README.md).
10: * Beyond code formatting, for the most part Envoy uses the
11:   [Google C++ style guidelines](https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.html).
12:   The following section covers the major areas where we deviate from the Google
13:   guidelines.
14: 
15: # Repository file layout
16: 
17: * Please see [REPO_LAYOUT.md](REPO_LAYOUT.md).
18: 
19: # Documentation
20: 
21: * If you are modifying the data plane structurally, please keep the [Life of a
22:   Request](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/intro/life_of_a_request) documentation up-to-date.
23: 
24: # Deviations from Google C++ style guidelines
25: 
26: * Exceptions are allowed on the control plane, though now discouraged in new code. Adding exceptions is disallowed on the data plane.
27: * References are always preferred over pointers when the reference cannot be null. This
28:   includes both const and non-const references.
29: * Function names should all use camel case starting with a lower case letter (e.g., `doFoo()`).
30: * Struct/Class member variables have a `_` postfix (e.g., `int foo_;`).
31: * Enum values using PascalCase (e.g., `RoundRobin`).
32: * 100 columns is the line limit.
33: * Use your GitHub name in TODO comments, e.g. `TODO(foobar): blah`.
34: * Smart pointers are type aliased:
35:   * `using FooPtr = std::unique_ptr<Foo>;`
36:   * `using BarSharedPtr = std::shared_ptr<Bar>;`
37:   * `using BlahConstSharedPtr = std::shared_ptr<const Blah>;`
38:   * Regular pointers (e.g. `int* foo`) should not be type aliased.
39: * `absl::optional<std::reference_wrapper<T>>` has a helper class in `envoy/common/optref.h`, and is type aliased:
40:   * `using FooOptRef = OptRef<T>;`
41:   * `using FooOptConstRef = OptRef<const T>;`
42: * If move semantics are intended, prefer specifying function arguments with `&&`.
43:   E.g., `void onHeaders(Http::HeaderMapPtr&& headers, ...)`. The rationale for this is that it
44:   forces the caller to specify `std::move(...)` or pass a temporary and makes the intention at
45:   the callsite clear. Otherwise, it's difficult to tell if a const reference is actually being
46:   passed to the called function. This is true even for `std::unique_ptr`.
47: * Prefer `unique_ptr` over `shared_ptr` wherever possible. `unique_ptr` makes ownership in
48:   production code easier to reason about. Note that this creates some test oddities where
49:   production code requires a `unique_ptr` but the test must still have access to the memory
50:   the production code is using (mock or otherwise). In these cases it is acceptable to allocate
51:   raw memory in a test and return it to the production code with the expectation that the
52:   production code will hold it in a `unique_ptr` and free it. Envoy uses the factory pattern
53:   quite a bit for these cases. (Search the code for "factory").
54: * Prefer explicitly sized integer types, such as uint64_t rather than size_t. In particular, use
55:   explicitly sized integers for data that is written to disk or involved in math that might overflow.
56: * The Google C++ style guide points out that [non-PoD static and global variables are forbidden](https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.html#Static_and_Global_Variables).
57:   This _includes_ types such as `std::string`. We encourage the use of the
58:   advice in the [C++ FAQ on the static initialization
59:   fiasco](https://isocpp.org/wiki/faq/ctors#static-init-order-on-first-use) for
60:   how to best handle this.
61: * The Google C++ style guide points out that [constant vars should be named `kConstantVar`](https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.html#Constant_Names).
62:   In the Envoy codebase we use `ConstantVar` or `CONSTANT_VAR`. If you pick `CONSTANT_VAR`,
63:   please be certain the name is globally significant to avoid potential conflicts with #defines,
64:   which are not namespace-scoped, and may appear in externally controlled header files.
65: * API-level comments should follow normal Doxygen conventions. Use `@param` to describe
66:   parameters and `@return <return-type>` for return values. Internal comments for
67:   methods and member variables may be regular C++ `//` comments or Doxygen at
68:   developer discretion. Where possible, methods should have meaningful
69:   documentation on expected input and state preconditions.
70: * Header guards should use `#pragma once`.
71: * All code should be inside a top-level Envoy namespace. There are some
72:   exceptions such as `main()` functions. When code cannot be placed inside the
73:   Envoy namespace there should be a comment of the form `// NOLINT(namespace-envoy)` at
74:   the top of the file.
75: * If a method that must be defined outside the `test` directory is intended to be called only
76:   from test code then it should have a name that ends in `ForTest()` such as `aMethodForTest()`.
77:   In most cases tests can and should be structured so this is not necessary.
78: * Tests default to StrictMock so will fail if hitting unexpected warnings. Feel free to use
79:   NiceMock for mocks whose behavior is not the focus of a test.
80: * [Thread
81:   annotations](https://github.com/abseil/abseil-cpp/blob/master/absl/base/thread_annotations.h),
82:   such as `ABSL_GUARDED_BY`, should be used for shared state guarded by
83:   locks/mutexes.
84: * Functions intended to be local to a cc file should be declared in an anonymous namespace,
85:   rather than using the 'static' keyword. Note that the
86:   [Google C++ style guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/cppguide.html#Unnamed_Namespaces_and_Static_Variables)
87:    allows either, but in Envoy we prefer anonymous namespaces.
88: * Braces are required for all control statements include single line if, while, etc. statements.
89: * Don't use [mangled Protobuf enum
90:   names](https://developers.google.com/protocol-buffers/docs/reference/cpp-generated#enum).
91: 
92: # Error handling
93: 
94: A few general notes on our error handling philosophy:
95: 
96: * All error code returns should be checked.
97: * At a very high level, our philosophy is that errors should be handled gracefully when caused by:
98:   - Untrusted network traffic (from downstream, upstream, or extensions like filters)
99:   - Raised by the Envoy process environment and are *likely* to happen
100:   - Third party dependency return codes
101: * Examples of likely environnmental errors include any type of network error, disk IO error, bad
102:   data returned by an API call, bad data read from runtime files, etc. This includes loading
103:   configuration at runtime.
104: * Third party dependency return codes should be checked and gracefully handled. Examples include
105:   HTTP/2 or JSON parsers. Some return codes may be handled by continuing, for example, in case of an
106:   out of process RPC failure.
107: * Testing should cover any serious cases that may result in infinite loops, crashes, or serious
108:   errors. Non-trivial invariants are also encouraged to have testing. Internal, localized invariants
109:   may not need testing.
110: * Errors in the Envoy environment that are *unlikely* to happen after process initialization, should
111:   lead to process death, under the assumption that the additional burden of defensive coding and
112:   testing is not an effective use of time for an error that should not happen given proper system
113:   setup. Examples of these types of errors include not being able to open the shared memory region,
114:   system calls that should not fail assuming correct parameters (which should be validated via
115:   tests), etc. Examples of system calls that should not fail when passed valid parameters include
116:   the kernel returning a valid `sockaddr` after a successful call to `accept()`, `pthread_create()`,
117:   `pthread_join()`, etc. However, system calls that require permissions may cause likely errors in
118:   some deployments and need graceful error handling.
119: * OOM events (both memory and FDs) or ENOMEM errors are considered fatal crashing errors. An OOM
120:   error should never silently be ignored and should crash the process either via the C++ allocation
121:   error exception, an explicit `RELEASE_ASSERT` following a third party library call, or an obvious
122:   crash on a subsequent line via null pointer dereference. This rule is again based on the
123:   philosophy that the engineering costs of properly handling these cases are not worth it. Time is
124:   better spent designing proper system controls that shed load if resource usage becomes too high,
125:   etc.
126: * The "less is more" error handling philosophy described in the previous points is primarily
127:   based on the fact that restarts are designed to be fast, reliable and cheap.
128: * Although we strongly recommend that any type of startup error leads to a fatal error, since this
129:   is almost always a result of faulty configuration which should be caught during a canary process,
130:   there may be cases in which we want some classes of startup errors to be non-fatal. For example,
131:   if a misconfigured option is not necessary for server operation. Although this is discouraged, we
132:   will discuss these on a case by case basis during code review (an example of this
133:   is the `--admin-address-path` option). **If degraded mode error handling is implemented, we require
134:   that there is complete test coverage for the degraded case.** Additionally, the user should be
135:   aware of the degraded state minimally via an error log of level warn or greater and via the
136:   increment of a stat.
137: * If you do need to log a non-fatal warning or error, you can unit-test it with EXPECT_LOG_CONTAINS
138:   or EXPECT_NO_LOGS from [logging.h](test/test_common/logging.h). It's generally bad practice to
139:   test by depending on log messages unless the actual behavior being validated is logging.
140:   It's preferable to export statistics to enable consumption by external monitoring for any
141:   behavior that should be externally consumed or to introduce appropriate internal interfaces
142:   such as mocks for internal behavior.
143: * The error handling philosophy described herein is based on the assumption that Envoy is deployed
144:   using industry best practices (primarily canary). Major and obvious errors should always be
145:   caught in canary. If a low rate error leads to periodic crash cycling when deployed to
146:   production, the error rate should allow for rollback without large customer impact.
147: * Tip: If the thought of adding the extra test coverage, logging, and stats to handle an error and
148:   continue seems ridiculous because *"this should never happen"*, it's a very good indication that
149:   the appropriate behavior is to terminate the process and not handle the error. When in doubt,
150:   please discuss.
151: 
152: # Macro Usage
153: 
154: * The following macros are available:
155:   - `RELEASE_ASSERT`: fatal check.
156:   - `ASSERT`: fatal check in debug-only builds. These should be used to document (and check in
157:     debug-only builds) program invariants.
158:   - `ENVOY_BUG`: logs and increments a stat in release mode, fatal check in debug builds. These
159:     should be used where it may be useful to detect if an efficient condition is violated in
160:     production (and fatal check in debug-only builds). This will also log a stack trace
161:     of the previous calls leading up to `ENVOY_BUG`.
162: 
163: * Sub-macros alias the macros above and can be used to annotate specific situations:
164:   - `ENVOY_BUG_ALPHA` (alias `ENVOY_BUG`): Used for alpha or rapidly changing protocols that need
165:   detectability on probable conditions or invariants.
166: 
167: * Per above it's acceptable to turn failures into crash semantics via `RELEASE_ASSERT(condition)` or
168:   `PANIC(message)` if there is no other sensible behavior, e.g. in OOM (memory/FD) scenarios.
169: * Do not `ASSERT` on conditions imposed by the external environment. Either add error handling
170:   (potentially with an `ENVOY_BUG` for detectability) or `RELEASE_ASSERT` if the condition indicates
171:   that the process is unrecoverable.
172: * Use `ASSERT` and `ENVOY_BUG` liberally, but do not use them for things that will crash in an obvious
173:   way in a subsequent line. E.g., do not do `ASSERT(foo != nullptr); foo->doSomething();`.
174: * Use `ASSERT`s for true invariants and well-defined conditions that are useful for tests,
175:   debug-only checks and documentation. They may be `ENVOY_BUG`s if performance allows, see point
176:   below.
177: * `ENVOY_BUG`s provide detectability and more confidence than an `ASSERT`. They are useful for
178:   non-trivial conditions, those with complex control flow, and rapidly changing protocols. Testing
179:   should be added to ensure that Envoy can continue to operate even if an `ENVOY_BUG` condition is
180:   violated.
181: * Annotate conditions with comments on belief or reasoning, for example `Condition is guaranteed by
182:   caller foo` or `Condition is likely to hold after processing through external library foo`.
183: * Macro usage should be understandable to a reader. Add comments if not. They should be robust to
184:   future changes.
185: * Note that there is a gray line between external environment failures and program invariant
186:   violations. For example, memory corruption due to a security issue (a bug, deliberate buffer
187:   overflow etc.) might manifest as a violation of program invariants or as a detectable condition in
188:   the external environment (e.g. some library returning a highly unexpected error code or buffer
189:   contents). Unfortunately no rule can cleanly cover when to use `RELEASE_ASSERT` vs. `ASSERT`. In
190:   general we view `ASSERT` as the common case and `RELEASE_ASSERT` as the uncommon case, but
191:   experience and judgment may dictate a particular approach depending on the situation. The risk of
192:   process death from `RELEASE_ASSERT` should be justified with the severity and possibility of the
193:   condition to avoid unintentional crashes. You may use the following guide:
194:     * If a violation is high risk (will cause a crash in subsequent data processing or indicates a
195:       failure state beyond recovery), use `RELEASE_ASSERT`.
196:     * If a violation is medium or low risk (Envoy can continue safely) and is not expensive,
197:       consider `ENVOY_BUG`.
198:     * Otherwise (if a condition is expensive or test-only), use `ASSERT`.
199: 
200: Below is a guideline for macro usage. The left side of the table has invariants and the right side
201: has error conditions that can be triggered and should be gracefully handled. `ENVOY_BUG` represents
202: a middle ground that can be used for uncertain conditions that need detectability. `ENVOY_BUG`s can
203: also be added for errors if they warrant detection.
204: 
205: | `ASSERT`/`RELEASE_ASSERT` | `ENVOY_BUG` | Error handling and Testing |
206: | --- | --- | --- |
207: | Low level invariants in data structures | | |
208: | Simple, provable internal class invariants | Complex, uncertain internal class invariants (e.g. need detectability if violated) | |
209: | Provable (pre/post)-conditions | Complicated but likely (pre-/post-) conditions that are low-risk (Envoy can continue safely) | Triggerable or uncertain conditions, may be based on untrusted data plane traffic or an extensions’ contract. |
210: |                                                                                     | Conditions in alpha or changing extensions that need detectability. (`ENVOY_BUG_ALPHA`) | |
211: | Unlikely environment errors after process initialization that would otherwise crash | | Likely environment errors, e.g. return codes from untrusted extensions, dependencies or system calls, network error, bad data read, permission based errors, etc. |
212: | Fatal crashing events. e.g. OOMs, deadlocks, no process recovery possible | | |
213: 
214: # Hermetic and deterministic tests
215: 
216: Tests should be hermetic, i.e. have all dependencies explicitly captured and not depend on the local
217: environment. In general, there should be no non-local network access. In addition:
218: 
219: * Port numbers should not be hardcoded. Tests should bind to port zero and then discover the bound
220:   port when needed. This avoids flakes due to conflicting ports and allows tests to be executed
221:   concurrently by Bazel. See
222:   [`test/integration/integration_test.h`](test/integration/integration_test.h) and
223:   [`test/common/network/listener_impl_test.cc`](test/common/network/listener_impl_test.cc)
224:   for examples of tests that do this.
225: 
226: * Paths should be constructed using:
227:   * The methods in [`TestEnvironment`](test/test_common/environment.h) for C++ tests.
228:   * With `${TEST_TMPDIR}` (for writable temporary space) or `${TEST_SRCDIR}` for read-only access to
229:     test inputs in shell tests.
230:   * With `{{ test_tmpdir }}`, `{{ test_rundir }}` and `{{ test_udsdir }}` respectively for JSON templates.
231:     `{{ test_udsdir }}` is provided for pathname based Unix Domain Sockets, which must fit within a
232:     108 character limit on Linux, a property that might not hold for `{{ test_tmpdir }}`.
233: 
234: Tests should be deterministic. They should not rely on randomness or details
235: such as the current time. Instead, mocks such as
236: [`MockRandomGenerator`](test/mocks/common.h) and
237: [`SimulatedTimeSystem`](test/test_common/simulated_time_system.h) should be used.
238: 
239: # Google style guides for other languages
240: 
241: * [Python](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html)
242: * [Bash](https://google.github.io/styleguide/shell.xml)
243: * [Bazel](https://bazel.build/versions/master/docs/skylark/build-style.html)
244: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/envoy--v1.32.1@b35dd17bea856e19fc4e65fb05e68170841d3726/-/blob/STYLE.md"}

{"content":"1: # Repository layout overview
2: 
3: This is a high level overview of how the repository is laid out to both aid in code investigation,
4: as well as to clearly specify how extensions are added to the repository. The top level directories
5: are:
6: 
7: * [.azure-pipelines/](.azure-pipelines/): Configuration for
8: [Azure Pipelines](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/devops/pipelines/).
9: * [api/](api/): Envoy data plane API.
10: * [bazel/](bazel/): Configuration for Envoy's use of [Bazel](https://bazel.build/).
11: * [ci/](ci/): Scripts used both during CI as well as to build Docker containers.
12: * [configs/](configs/): Example Envoy configurations.
13: * [docs/](docs/): End user facing Envoy proxy and data plane API documentation as well as scripts
14:   for publishing final docs during releases.
15: * [examples/](examples/): Larger Envoy examples using Docker and Docker Compose.
16: * [envoy/](envoy/): "Public" interface headers for "core" Envoy. In general,
17:   these are almost entirely 100% abstract classes. There are a few cases of not-abstract classes in
18:   the "public" headers, typically for performance reasons. Note that "core" includes some
19:   "extensions" such as the HTTP connection manager filter and associated functionality which are
20:   so fundamental to Envoy that they will likely never be optional from a compilation perspective.
21: * [restarter/](restarter/): Envoy's hot restart wrapper Python script.
22: * [security/](security/): Some templates for reporting security issues of Envoy. Historical security issues can also be found here.
23: * [source/](source/): Source code for core Envoy as well as extensions. The layout of this directory
24:   is discussed in further detail below.
25: * [support/](support/): Development support scripts (pre-commit Git hooks, etc.)
26: * [test/](test/): Test code for core Envoy as well as extensions. The layout of this directory is
27:   discussed in further detail below.
28: * [tools/](tools/): Miscellaneous tools that have not found a home somewhere else.
29: 
30: ## [source/](source/)
31: 
32: * [common/](source/common/): Core Envoy code (not specific to extensions) that is also not
33:   specific to a standalone server implementation. I.e., this is the code that could be used if Envoy
34:   were eventually embedded as a library.
35: * [docs/](source/docs/): Miscellaneous developer/design documentation that is not relevant for
36:   the public user documentation.
37: * [exe/](source/exe/): Code specific to building the final production Envoy server binary. This is
38:   the only code that is not shared by integration and unit tests.
39: * [extensions/](source/extensions/): Extensions to the core Envoy code. The layout of this
40:   directory is discussed in further detail below.
41: * [server/](source/server/): Code specific to running Envoy as a standalone server. E.g,
42:   configuration, server startup, workers, etc. Over time, the line between `common/` and `server/`
43:   has become somewhat blurred. Use best judgment as to where to place something.
44: 
45: ## [test/](test/)
46: 
47: Not every directory within test is described below, but a few highlights:
48: 
49: * Unit tests are found in directories matching their [source/](source/) equivalents. E.g.,
50:   [common/](test/common/), [exe/](test/exe/), and [server/](test/server/).
51: * Extension unit tests also match their source equivalents in [extensions/](test/extensions/).
52: * [integration/](test/integration/) holds end-to-end integration tests using roughly the real
53:   Envoy server code, fake downstream clients, and fake upstream servers. Integration tests also
54:   test some of the extensions found in the repository. Note that in the future, we would like to
55:   allow integration tests that are specific to extensions and are not required for covering
56:   "core" Envoy functionality. Those integration tests will likely end up in the
57:   [extensions/](test/extensions/) directory but further work and thinking is required before
58:   we get to that point.
59: * [mocks/](test/mocks/) contains mock implementations of all of the core Envoy interfaces found in
60:   [include/](include/).
61: * Other directories include tooling used for configuration testing, coverage testing, fuzz testing,
62:   common test code, etc.
63: 
64: ## [source/extensions](source/extensions/) layout
65: 
66: We maintain a very specific code and namespace layout for extensions. This aids in discovering
67: code/extensions, and allows us specify extension owners in [CODEOWNERS](CODEOWNERS).
68: 
69: * All extensions are either registered in [all_extensions.bzl](source/extensions/all_extensions.bzl)
70:   or [extensions_build_config.bzl](source/extensions/extensions_build_config.bzl). The former is
71:   for extensions that cannot be removed from the primary Envoy build. The latter is for extensions
72:   that can be removed on a site specific basis. See [bazel/README.md](bazel/README.md) for how to
73:   compile out extensions on a site specific basis. Note that by default extensions should be
74:   removable from the build unless there is a very good reason.
75: * These are the top level extension directories and associated namespaces:
76:   * [access_loggers/](/source/extensions/access_loggers): Access log implementations which use
77:     the `Envoy::Extensions::AccessLoggers` namespace.
78:   * [bootstrap/](/source/extensions/bootstrap): Bootstrap extensions which use
79:     the `Envoy::Extensions::Bootstrap` namespace.
80:   * [clusters/](/source/extensions/clusters): Cluster extensions which use the
81:     `Envoy::Extensions::Clusters` namespace.
82:   * [compression/](/source/extensions/compression): Compression extensions
83:     which use `Envoy::Extensions::Compression` namespace.
84:   * [fatal_actions/](/source/extensions/fatal_actions): Fatal Action extensions
85:     which use the `Envoy::Extensions::FatalActions` namespace.
86:   * [filters/http/](/source/extensions/filters/http): HTTP L7 filters which use the
87:     `Envoy::Extensions::HttpFilters` namespace.
88:   * [filters/listener/](/source/extensions/filters/listener): Listener filters which use the
89:     `Envoy::Extensions::ListenerFilters` namespace.
90:   * [filters/network/](/source/extensions/filters/network): L4 network filters which use the
91:     `Envoy::Extensions::NetworkFilters` namespace.
92:   * [formatters/](/source/extensions/formatters): Access log formatters which use the
93:     `Envoy::Extensions::Formatters` namespace.
94:   * [grpc_credentials/](/source/extensions/grpc_credentials): Custom gRPC credentials which use the
95:     `Envoy::Extensions::GrpcCredentials` namespace.
96:   * [health_checker/](/source/extensions/health_checker): Custom health checkers which use the
97:     `Envoy::Extensions::HealthCheckers` namespace.
98:   * [internal_redirect/](/source/extensions/internal_redirect): Internal Redirect
99:     extensions which use the `Envoy::Extensions::InternalRedirect` namespace.
100:   * [quic_listeners/](/source/extensions/quic_listeners): QUIC extensions which
101:     use the `Envoy::Quic` namespace.
102:   * [resource_monitors/](/source/extensions/resource_monitors): Resource monitor
103:     extensions which use the `Envoy::Extensions::ResourceMonitors` namespace.
104:   * [retry/](/source/extensions/retry): Retry extensions which use the
105:     `Envoy::Extensions::Retry` namespace.
106:   * [stat_sinks/](/source/extensions/stat_sinks): Stat sink implementations which use the
107:     `Envoy::Extensions::StatSinks` namespace.
108:   * [tracers/](/source/extensions/tracers): Tracers which use the
109:     `Envoy::Extensions::Tracers` namespace.
110:   * [transport_sockets/](/source/extensions/transport_sockets): Transport socket implementations
111:     which use the `Envoy::Extensions::TransportSockets` namespace.
112:   * [upstreams/](/source/extensions/upstreams): Upstream extensions use the
113:     `Envoy::Extensions::Upstreams` namespace.
114:   * [watchdog/](/source/extensions/watchdog): Watchdog extensions use the
115:     `Envoy::Extensions::Watchdog` namespace.
116:   * [rate_limit_descriptors/](/source/extensions/rate_limit_descriptors): Rate limit
117:     descriptor extensions use the `Envoy::Extensions::RateLimitDescriptors`
118:     namespace.
119: * Each extension is contained wholly in its own namespace. E.g.,
120:   `Envoy::Extensions::NetworkFilters::Echo`.
121: * Common code that is used by multiple extensions should be in a `common/` directory as close to
122:   the extensions as possible. E.g., [filters/common/](/source/extensions/filters/common) for common
123:   code that is used by both HTTP and network filters. Common code used only by two HTTP filters
124:   would be found in `filters/http/common/`. Common code should be placed in a common namespace.
125:   E.g., `Envoy::Extensions::Filters::Common`.
126: 
127: ## [contrib](contrib/) layout
128: 
129: This directory contains contrib extensions. See [EXTENSION_POLICY.md](EXTENSION_POLICY.md) for
130: more information.
131: 
132: * [contrib/exe/](contrib/exe/): The default executable for contrib. This is similar to the
133:   `envoy-static` target but also includes all contrib extensions, and is used to produce the
134:   contrib image targets.
135: * [contrib/...](contrib/): The rest of this directory mirrors the [source/extensions](source/extensions/)
136:   layout. Contrib extensions are placed here.
137: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/envoy--v1.32.1@b35dd17bea856e19fc4e65fb05e68170841d3726/-/blob/REPO_LAYOUT.md"}

{"content":"1: # Writing new tests
2: 
3: The Envoy integration test framework is designed to make it simple to test downstream-Envoy-upstream
4: communication. In the common case, one
5: 
6: - Sends a request from downstream through Envoy
7: - Verifies the request is received upstream, and possibly inspects elements of the headers or body
8: - Sends a response from upstream through Envoy
9: - Verifies the request is received downstream, again possibly inspecting headers and body
10: 
11: For the simplest variant of this, one could do the following.
12: 
13: ```c++
14: // start Envoy, set up the fake upstreams.
15: initialize();
16: 
17: // Create a client aimed at Envoy’s default HTTP port.
18: codec_client_ = makeHttpConnection(makeClientConnection((lookupPort("http"))));
19: 
20: // Create some request headers.
21: Http::TestRequestHeaderMapImpl request_headers{{":method", "GET"},
22:                                                {":path", "/test/long/url"},
23:                                                {":scheme", "http"},
24:                                                {":authority", "host"}};
25: 
26: // Send the request headers from the client, wait until they are received upstream. When they
27: // are received, send the default response headers from upstream and wait until they are
28: // received at by client
29: auto response = sendRequestAndWaitForResponse(request_headers, 0, default_response_headers_, 0);
30: 
31: // Verify the proxied request was received upstream, as expected.
32: EXPECT_TRUE(upstream_request_->complete());
33: EXPECT_EQ(0U, upstream_request_->bodyLength());
34: // Verify the proxied response was received downstream, as expected.
35: EXPECT_TRUE(response->complete());
36: EXPECT_STREQ("200", response->headers().Status()->value().c_str());
37: EXPECT_EQ(0U, response_->body().size());
38: ```
39: 
40: Once you have the basic end-to-end test, it is fairly straight forward to modify it to test more
41: interesting corner cases. There are existing tests which send requests with bodies, have
42: downstream or upstream disconnect or time out, send multiple simultaneous requests on the same
43: connection, etc. Given that many of the disconnect/timeout cases are covered, a common case for
44: testing is covering a newly added configuration option.
45: 
46: Most of Envoy's tests have been migrated from using [`json flatfiles`](../config/integration/) to
47: using a basic configuration defined as `basic_config` in [`config/utility.cc`](../config/utility.cc).
48: This configuration may be modified before the call to `initialize()`.
49: The [`ConfigHelper`](../config/utility.h) has utilities for common alterations such as:
50: 
51: ```c++
52: // Set the default protocol to HTTP2
53: setDownstreamProtocol(Http::CodecType::HTTP2);
54: ```
55: 
56: or
57: 
58: ```c++
59: // Add a buffering filter on the request path
60: config_helper_.prependFilter(ConfigHelper::DEFAULT_BUFFER_FILTER);
61: ```
62: 
63: For other edits which are less likely reusable, one can add config modifiers. Config modifiers
64: allow arbitrary modification of Envoy’s configuration just before Envoy is brought up. One can add
65: a config modifier to alter the bootstrap proto, one which affects the first `HttpConnectionManager`
66: object in the config, or mix and match. Config modifiers are operated on in the order they are
67: added, so one could, for example, modify the default `HttpConnectionManager`, duplicate the listening
68: config, and then change the first `HttpConnectionManager` to be different from the second.
69: 
70: An example of modifying the bootstrap proto to overwrite runtime defaults:
71: 
72: ```c++
73: config_helper_.addRuntimeOverride("Foo", "bar");
74: });
75: ```
76: 
77: An example of modifying `HttpConnectionManager` to change Envoy’s HTTP/1.1 processing:
78: 
79: ```c++
80: config_helper_.addConfigModifier([&](envoy::extensions::filters::network::http_connection_manager::v3::HttpConnectionManager& hcm) -> void {
81:   nvoy::config::core::v3::Http1ProtocolOptions options;
82:   options.mutable_allow_absolute_url()->set_value(true);
83:   hcm.mutable_http_protocol_options()->CopyFrom(options);
84: };);
85: ```
86: 
87: An example of modifying `HttpConnectionManager` to add an additional upstream
88: cluster:
89: 
90: ```c++
91:    config_helper_.addConfigModifier([](envoy::config::bootstrap::v3::Bootstrap& bootstrap) {
92:       bootstrap.mutable_rate_limit_service()->set_cluster_name("ratelimit");
93:       auto* ratelimit_cluster = bootstrap.mutable_static_resources()->add_clusters();
94:       ratelimit_cluster->MergeFrom(bootstrap.static_resources().clusters()[0]);
95:       ratelimit_cluster->set_name("ratelimit");
96:       ConfigHelper::setHttp2(*ratelimit_cluster);
97:     });
98: ```
99: 
100: In addition to the existing test framework, which allows for carefully timed interaction and ordering of events between downstream, Envoy, and Upstream, there is now an “autonomous” framework which simplifies the common case where the timing is not essential (or bidirectional streaming is desired). When AutonomousUpstream is used, by setting `autonomous_upstream_ = true` before `initialize()`, upstream will by default create AutonomousHttpConnections for each incoming connection and AutonomousStreams for each incoming stream. By default, the streams will respond to each complete request with “200 OK” and 10 bytes of payload, but this behavior can be altered by setting various request headers, as documented in [`autonomous_upstream.h`](autonomous_upstream.h)
101: 
102: ## Common Problems
103: - If a response body length does not match the `content-length` header, any mock calls to wait for the response completion such as `sendRequestAndWaitForResponse` or `response_->waitForEndStream` could time out. Also, any asserts that the response was completed such as `EXPECT_TRUE(response_->complete())` could fail. Make sure that the response body length matches the `content-length` header.
104: 
105: # Extending the test framework
106: 
107: The Envoy integration test framework is most definitely a work in progress.
108: When encountering features which do not exist (actions for the autonomous
109: backend, testing new sections of configuration) please use your best judgement
110: if the changes will be needed by one specific test file, or will be likely
111: reused in other integration tests. If it's likely be reused, please add the
112: appropriate functions to existing utilities or add new test utilities. If it's
113: likely a one-off change, it can be scoped to the existing test file.
114: 
115: # Debugging integration tests
116: 
117: The Envoy integration test framework is generally designed to fast-fail when
118: things go wrong, with an explanatory message such as
119: "Timed out waiting for new connection."
120: but it's not always clear what the underlying cause is. Because there are many
121: Envoy components under test, often the best tool for debugging is to try to get
122: a run of the test with `--test_arg="-l debug"`, or `--test_arg="-l trace"` ideally
123: with a clean run (if it's a code change which caused test breakage) or a comparable
124: test (if it's a new test failing). Looking at the conn_manager_impl.cc
125: logs, router.cc logs, and fake_upstream.cc logs, can often give you a feel for
126: what unexpected event is occurring. If that doesn't help, following the GDB
127: instructions to run the test in a debugger or sprinkling cerrs around are both often helpful.
128: 
129: # Deflaking tests
130: 
131: The instructions below assume the developer is running tests natively with bazel
132: rather than in docker. For developers using docker the best workaround today is
133: to replace `//test/...` on the relevant `ci/do_ci.sh`with the command lines
134: referenced below and remember to back those changes out before sending the fix
135: upstream!
136: 
137: ## Reproducing test flakes
138: 
139: The first step of fixing test flakes is reproducing the test flake. In general
140: if you have written a test which flakes, you can start by running
141: 
142: ``
143: bazel test [test_name] --runs_per_test=1000
144: ``
145: 
146: Which runs the full test many times. If this works, great!  If not, it's worth
147: trying to stress your system more by running more tests in parallel, by setting
148: `--jobs` and `--local_resources.`
149: 
150: Once you've managed to reproduce a failure it may be beneficial to limit your
151: test run to the specific failing test(s) with `--gtest_filter`. This may cause
152: the test to flake less often (i.e. if two tests are interfering with each other,
153: scoping to your specific test name may harm rather than help reproducibility.)
154: but if it works it lets you iterate faster.
155: 
156: Another helpful tip for debugging is turn turn up Envoy trace logs with
157: `--test_arg="-l trace"`. Again if the test failure is due to a race, this may make
158: it harder to reproduce, and it may also hide any custom logging you add, but it's a
159: handy thing to know of to follow the general flow.
160: 
161: The full command might look something like
162: 
163: ```
164: bazel test //test/integration:http2_upstream_integration_test \
165: --test_arg=--gtest_filter="IpVersions/Http2UpstreamIntegrationTest.RouterRequestAndResponseWithBodyNoBuffer/IPv6" \
166: --jobs 60 --local_test_jobs=60 --runs_per_test=1000 --test_arg="-l trace"
167: ```
168: 
169: For hard to reproduce flakes, a sometimes useful tool is `stress`, available via
170: `apt install stress`. Running stress alongsize `bazel test` (in another window,
171: starting it after the build completes) can be a great help in reproducing issues,
172: especially where tests are blocked on different things. For example, if a test
173: spends some time blocked on network traffic and has a cpu race, running many
174: instances of the test doesn't much help repro because the other instances aren't
175: using cpu at the critical moment. For this case, `stress -c [number of cores]`
176: can frequently make a 1/1000 flake into a 1/2 flake. Less commonly, for example,
177: unusually slow disk access can flake a test, in which case `stress --hdd 8`
178: helps boost the failure rate.
179: 
180: ## Debugging test flakes
181: 
182: Once you've managed to reproduce your test flake, you get to figure out what's
183: going on. If your failure mode isn't documented below, ideally some combination
184: of cerr << logging and trace logs will help you sort out what is going on (and
185: please add to this document as you figure it out!)
186:
